{{nihongo|'''Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko'''|波多国造|''hata no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''hata kokuzō''}} was a [[kuni no miyatsuko]] who ruled the western portion of [[Tosa Province]] under the later {{ill|ritsuryō provinces|ja|令制国}}, corresponding to the western part of modern [[Kōchi Prefecture]].

According to the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]], Karaso-no-Mikoto was appointed kuni no miyatsuko by divine instruction.

The Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko is thought to have ruled {{ill|Hata District|ja|幡多郡}} within the later ritsuryō province of Tosa. There are two theories regarding its center: one places it in modern Nakamura in {{ill|Shimanto, Kōchi|ja|四万十市|lt=Shimanto}} city, while another points to the area of the Hirata kofun group, including the {{ill|Hirata Sogayama Kofun|ja|平田曽我山古墳}}, at Hirata in {{ill|Sukumo, Kōchi|ja|宿毛市|lt=Sukumo}} city. The {{ill|Tosa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|都佐国造}} that appears in the "Kokuzō Hongi" is considered to have been established later than the Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko; moreover, before the {{ill|ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} system, the [[Nankaidō]] road ran from [[Iyo Province|Iyo]] through this area to the territory of the Tosa no Kuni no Miyatsuko,<ref>The {{ill|official road|ja|官道|lt=kandō}} to Tosa Province, the Nankaidō, passed through Iyo Province, but because the route was long and the mountains rugged, it was rerouted through [[Awa Province (Tokushima)|Awa Province]] in {{ill|Yōrō|ja|養老}} 2 (718) (''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', entry for Yōrō 2, 5th month, 7th day).</ref> so the area is said to have been more advanced than central Tosa.

== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}

== References ==
* Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Hensan Iinkai (ed.), ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten (Kōchi-ken)'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1991, ISBN 4-04-001390-5, pp. 808, 1075, 1111.

{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:はたのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:土佐国]]
[[Category:高知県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:波多国造]]
